articles.headline: Sinaloa Cartel Civil War Turns Culiacán Into a Kill Zone
articles.subheadline: Eighteen months of factional fighting have driven homicides up over 70% as paramedics, mothers, and journalists navigate daily carnage
articles.body:
The Mexican city of Culiacán, capital of the northern state of Sinaloa, has descended into sustained urban warfare as rival factions of the Sinaloa cartel wage a bloody internal conflict now stretching past 18 months — with no end in sight.

The violence traces back to the capture and removal of longtime cartel co-leader Ismael "El Mayo" Zambada, who was lured to the United States in an elaborate sting operation and now sits in a U.S. prison. His absence fractured the organization that once operated as a unified criminal empire, splitting it into warring camps led by competing lieutenants and family members. Paramedic call-outs in Culiacán surged more than 70 percent last year, with emergency responders reporting that nearly every incident they attend ends with a fatality rather than a rescue. Paramedics now wear 14 kilograms of Kevlar body armor to crime scenes, uncertain whether assailants remain nearby.

The scale of brutality has shocked even hardened observers. Bodies bearing signs of torture — skulls flayed, eyes removed — have been dumped outside shopping malls and along major highways, accompanied by written warnings from one faction to another. A 16-year-old boy was shot dead at close range while riding his bicycle, his body surrounded by more than a dozen shell casings. Schools, hospitals, and funerals have all been targeted. Veteran local journalist Ernesto Martínez, who has covered cartel violence for 27 years, said the city averages five to six homicides per day despite the presence of thousands of Mexican troops and military checkpoints on most roads.

The crisis comes as broader geopolitical pressure mounts on Mexico. U.S. President Donald Trump has designated the Sinaloa cartel and other trafficking organizations as terrorist groups, labeled fentanyl a weapon of mass destruction, and threatened direct military action if Mexico fails to curb the drug trade. The cartel built its fortune largely on fentanyl production, flooding American streets with synthetic opioids that have killed tens of thousands. Meanwhile, the death of Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes — "El Mencho," leader of the rival Jalisco New Generation Cartel — in custody on Sunday after a firefight in Jalisco state underscores a recurring pattern: the removal of a powerful cartel boss often intensifies rather than diminishes violence as subordinates scramble for dominance.

On the ground in Culiacán, BBC correspondent Quentin Sommerville met armed members of one Sinaloa faction who offered a chilling prescription for peace — the government should stand aside and let them kill each other until only one group remains. "A lot of people will keep dying because the cartel is still fighting," one masked fighter said. "The war will continue. Nothing will calm down until there's only one faction left." For civilians, the toll extends beyond the dead to the disappeared. Reynalda Pulido, whose son Javier Ernesto vanished in December 2020, leads a group called Mothers Fighting Back. Accompanied by armed military escorts, the women probe fields with metal rods and shovels, smelling disturbed soil for the distinctive odor of human remains — searching for closure in a city where fear, as one paramedic put it, is everywhere and constant.

articles.headline: Machine Learning Tackles Inverse Design of Exotic Metamaterials
articles.subheadline: Neural network models can now predict and reverse-engineer lattice structures with negative thermal expansion and negative Poisson's ratios
articles.body:
Researchers have developed a suite of machine learning models capable of designing metamaterials that simultaneously exhibit two counterintuitive physical properties — negative Poisson's ratios and negative thermal expansion — a combination that could prove transformative for aerospace, precision instruments, and other engineering applications where thermal stability is critical.

Xiang-Long Peng and Bai-Xiang Xu, in a paper posted to the arXiv preprint server on February 12, describe a framework that flips the traditional materials design process on its head. Rather than painstakingly tweaking a structure's geometry and hoping it yields desired thermoelastic behavior, their inverse models accept target properties as inputs and output the specific geometrical features and base material combinations needed to achieve them. The approach centers on bimaterial hybrid-honeycomb lattice structures — periodic arrangements of two different materials whose interplay gives rise to properties no single material possesses.

The foundation of the work is a large dataset generated through computational homogenization, systematically varying the geometrical parameters and constituent material properties of the lattice structures. A forward neural network model was first trained on this dataset, learning to predict effective thermoelastic properties — including coefficients of thermal expansion and Poisson's ratios — given a specific structural design. This forward model demonstrated high accuracy and replaced expensive finite-element simulations, enabling rapid property evaluation. The researchers then built six distinct inverse models, each tailored to different design scenarios defined by varying combinations of target properties and design variables. Critically, the trained forward model was embedded directly into the loss functions of the inverse models, ensuring that suggested designs would actually produce the desired behavior.

Materials with negative Poisson's ratios, known as auxetics, contract laterally when compressed rather than bulging outward — a property useful for impact resistance and shape-morphing structures. Negative thermal expansion materials, meanwhile, shrink when heated instead of expanding, making them valuable for applications requiring dimensional stability across temperature swings. Achieving both properties simultaneously in a single structure opens possibilities for components that maintain precise geometries under mechanical and thermal loads, a persistent challenge in satellite structures, optical systems, and microelectronics packaging.

The demonstrated framework represents a broader trend in materials science where machine learning is accelerating the discovery and optimization of architected materials. By reducing the computational cost of exploring vast design spaces, approaches like this one could shorten the path from concept to application for metamaterials that have long remained laboratory curiosities. The researchers note that their models can be applied directly to practical inverse design tasks in novel engineering systems, suggesting the work is oriented toward near-term deployment rather than purely academic exploration.

articles.headline: Ancient Microbial Life Could Endure 50 Million Years in Martian Ice
articles.subheadline: NASA and Penn State experiments show pure ice shields organic molecules from cosmic radiation, reshaping Mars exploration strategy
articles.body:
Laboratory experiments simulating Martian conditions have demonstrated that amino acids — the fundamental building blocks of proteins — can survive more than 50 million years when frozen in pure water ice, even under relentless bombardment by cosmic radiation. The findings, published in the journal Astrobiology, could fundamentally redirect where future Mars missions search for evidence of life.

Researchers from NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center and Penn State University sealed E. coli bacteria in test tubes of pure water ice, then subjected them to gamma radiation at minus 60 degrees Fahrenheit — conditions designed to replicate the Martian surface. The radiation dose simulated 20 million years of cosmic ray exposure, with mathematical modeling extending the projection to 50 million years. More than 10 percent of the amino acids in pure ice samples survived the full simulated duration. That timescale far exceeds the estimated age of some current Martian surface ice deposits, which are often less than two million years old, meaning any organic traces locked within could still be intact.

The study, led by NASA Goddard space scientist Alexander Pavlov, produced a counterintuitive result. When ice was mixed with silicate-based rocks and clays typical of Martian sediment, organic material degraded roughly ten times faster. A 2022 study by the same team had suggested that water ice alone would accelerate destruction even further, but the opposite proved true. Researchers believe a thin film forms at the boundary where ice contacts mineral grains, allowing radiation-generated reactive particles to move more freely and attack organic compounds. In solid, uncontaminated ice, those harmful particles appear to become frozen in place, unable to reach and damage nearby molecules.

"That means if there are bacteria near the surface of Mars, future missions can find it," said Christopher House, a Penn State geosciences professor and co-author of the study. House noted that while Mars harbors abundant ice, most of it lies just below the surface, requiring missions equipped with drills or powerful scoops — capabilities demonstrated by NASA's 2008 Phoenix lander, which first excavated and photographed subsurface Martian ice.

The implications extend beyond Mars. The team also tested organic preservation at the far colder temperatures found on Europa, Jupiter's ice-covered moon, and Saturn's Enceladus. Degradation slowed even further in those conditions — an encouraging sign for NASA's Europa Clipper mission, which launched in 2024 and is expected to reach Jupiter by 2030 to conduct 49 close flybys of Europa's ice shell. Taken together, the results suggest that ice across the solar system may function as a biological time capsule, and that the search for extraterrestrial life should prioritize pristine frozen deposits over the rocks and soils that have long been the primary targets of planetary exploration.

articles.headline: Ex-L3Harris Executive Sentenced to 87 Months for Selling Zero-Day Exploits to Russia
articles.subheadline: Peter Williams received $1.3 million in cryptocurrency for hacking tools that could compromise millions of devices worldwide
articles.body:
A former senior executive at U.S. defense contractor L3Harris has been sentenced to more than seven years in federal prison after admitting he stole and sold sophisticated hacking tools to a Russian exploit broker while Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine was underway.

Peter Williams, 39, an Australian citizen who lived in Washington, D.C., served as general manager of Trenchant — L3Harris's secretive division responsible for developing offensive cyber capabilities for the U.S. government and allied intelligence services. Between 2022 and 2025, prosecutors say, Williams exploited his unrestricted access to the company's secure networks to download zero-day exploits onto a portable hard drive, then sold them to Operation Zero, a firm the U.S. government describes as "one of the world's most nefarious exploit brokers," in exchange for $1.3 million in cryptocurrency. He used the proceeds to purchase a house, jewelry, and luxury watches.

Zero-day exploits — techniques that weaponize software vulnerabilities unknown to their developers — are among the most prized assets in the intelligence world, with individual exploits sometimes commanding millions of dollars. The Department of Justice alleged that the tools Williams leaked could have enabled access to "millions of computers and devices around the world," likely targeting flaws in widely used consumer platforms such as Apple's iOS, Google's Android, and popular web browsers. L3Harris estimated the loss at $35 million, though the company said the stolen tools were not classified as government secrets. The question of whether Apple, Google, or other affected vendors were ever notified about the compromised vulnerabilities remains unanswered — a significant concern given that the exploits may still be usable against unsuspecting consumers.

The case's geopolitical dimensions sharpened on the same day as Williams's sentencing, when the U.S. Treasury Department imposed sanctions on Operation Zero and its founder, Sergey Zelenyuk, formally designating the company a national security threat. The Treasury disclosed that Operation Zero had resold the stolen tools to "at least one unauthorized user," whose identity has not been publicly revealed. Intriguingly, the sanctions announcement also targeted an alleged member of the Trickbot cybercrime gang who purportedly worked with Operation Zero, raising the possibility that the exploits may have reached criminal networks as well as state actors. Operation Zero, which openly claims to work with the Russian government, has publicly advertised bounties of millions of dollars for mobile exploits, specifying that its "end user is a non-NATO country."

Williams, who previously served in Australia's military and worked at the country's top foreign signals intelligence agency, contacted Operation Zero under a pseudonym, and it remains unclear whether the Russian firm ever knew his true identity. Prosecutors described his actions as a betrayal of the United States. His 87-month sentence marks one of the most significant prosecutions involving the leak of Western-made offensive cyber tools, underscoring the persistent insider threat facing defense contractors entrusted with some of the digital world's most dangerous weapons.

articles.headline: Nvidia Earnings Loom as Critical Test for AI-Driven Market Rally
articles.subheadline: Wednesday's report arrives as investor anxiety mounts over the sustainability of artificial intelligence spending
articles.body:
Nvidia Corp. is set to report quarterly earnings on Wednesday afternoon, a moment that carries outsized significance for a U.S. stock market that has become deeply intertwined with the fortunes of artificial intelligence.

The chipmaker's results land at a particularly fraught juncture. Investors who have poured trillions of dollars into AI-linked equities over the past two years are showing growing signs of impatience, demanding concrete evidence that the massive capital expenditures flowing into GPU infrastructure will translate into sustainable revenue and profits across the technology sector. Any hint of softening demand, margin compression, or tempered forward guidance from Nvidia could ripple well beyond the company's own share price and into the broader indices where mega-cap tech stocks hold enormous weight.

Nvidia has become the single most consequential earnings report on Wall Street, a status earned by its dominant position in the data center GPU market that underpins virtually all large-scale AI training and inference workloads. The company's revenue trajectory — which saw explosive growth as hyperscalers like Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta raced to build out AI capacity — has been the primary engine of the market's AI narrative. But that same concentration of expectations means the stock is priced for near-flawless execution, leaving little room for disappointment.

The nervousness reflects a broader recalibration underway across markets. After a period of largely uncritical enthusiasm for anything AI-related, investors are scrutinizing whether the technology's economic returns justify the hundreds of billions of dollars being committed to infrastructure buildouts. Questions about the pace of enterprise AI adoption, competition from custom chips developed by Nvidia's own customers, and potential supply chain complications have all contributed to a more cautious tone heading into the report.

For the market at large, Wednesday's after-hours release will function as a referendum on the AI investment thesis that has driven much of the equity rally. A strong beat and robust outlook could reinforce confidence and extend gains, while a miss — or even guidance that merely meets expectations — risks triggering a reassessment that extends far beyond Nvidia's own $2-trillion-plus market capitalization.

articles.headline: Tether Pours $200M Into Digital Marketplace Whop at $1.6B Valuation
articles.subheadline: The stablecoin giant aims to embed USDT payments into a fast-growing creator economy platform with 18.4 million users
articles.body:
Tether, the company behind the world's largest stablecoin USDT, is making its biggest push yet into consumer-facing commerce with a $200 million investment in Whop, an online marketplace for digital goods and creator services. The deal values the startup at $1.6 billion, according to Whop CEO Steven Schwartz.

Whop operates a digital marketplace where creators sell access to software tools, trading communities, online courses, and other digital products. The platform claims 18.4 million users and approximately $3 billion in annual creator earnings, with gross transaction volume growing roughly 25% month-over-month — a pace that, if sustained, would represent extraordinary scaling in the creator economy space.

Under the terms of the partnership, Whop will integrate Tether's crypto wallet infrastructure directly into its platform, enabling users and creators to hold, transact, and settle payments in stablecoins including USDT and Tether's newer U.S.-focused USAT token. The integration is designed to let creators accept digital dollar payments and settle globally without relying on traditional banks or card networks — a proposition with particular appeal in markets where banking access is limited or fees are prohibitive.

The capital injection will fund Whop's international expansion into Latin America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific, regions where Tether's $185 billion USDT token already sees heavy adoption as a vehicle for accessing U.S. dollar-denominated transactions. Whop also plans to introduce lending and borrowing tools built on decentralized finance infrastructure, broadening its financial services beyond simple payments.

The investment signals a strategic evolution for Tether, which has historically derived its dominance from crypto trading activity and cross-border remittances. By embedding its stablecoins into a rapidly growing marketplace platform, the company is positioning USDT as everyday commercial infrastructure rather than merely a crypto trading instrument. If Whop's growth trajectory holds, the deal could serve as a template for how stablecoin issuers intend to compete with traditional payment rails in the broader digital economy.

articles.headline: Data Center Backlash Intensifies as States Weigh Construction Bans
articles.subheadline: From New York to New Orleans, a growing wave of moratoriums threatens Big Tech's $650 billion infrastructure buildout
articles.body:
A rising tide of public opposition to data center construction is sweeping across the United States, with communities and state legislatures moving to pause or restrict the massive infrastructure buildout fueling the artificial intelligence boom.

New York became the latest flashpoint earlier this month when State Senator Liz Krueger and Assemblymember Anna Kelles introduced legislation that would impose a three-year moratorium on new data center permits statewide — what the lawmakers called the "strongest" such proposal in the country. The bill would buy time for regulators to assess the environmental and economic toll the facilities are exacting on local communities. New York joins a rapidly expanding list of jurisdictions pumping the brakes: New Orleans passed a one-year construction moratorium in recent weeks, Madison, Wisconsin enacted a similar freeze in January, and numerous communities across Georgia and Michigan have adopted comparable restrictions.

The opposition spans the political spectrum. In Florida, Governor Ron DeSantis unveiled an AI "bill of rights" empowering communities to limit data center development. Vermont Senator Bernie Sanders has called for a nationwide moratorium. Arizona Governor Katie Hobbs has endorsed stripping the industry of tax incentives. The backlash is fueled by mounting concerns over energy affordability, water consumption, noise pollution, and strain on local infrastructure. In New York, 30 state lawmakers recently urged Governor Kathy Hochul to declare an "energy state of emergency" amid soaring utility rates — a crisis that data center growth is widely seen as worsening.

The political headwinds arrive at a precarious moment for the technology sector. Amazon, Google, Meta, and Microsoft collectively plan to spend approximately $650 billion in capital expenditures over the next year, with the overwhelming majority directed toward data center expansion. Yet polling suggests that momentum is shifting against them: an Echelon Insights survey found 46 percent of respondents would oppose a data center being built in their community, compared with just 35 percent in favor. A separate Politico poll indicated that many voters remain undecided, leaving public sentiment up for grabs.

Tech companies are responding on multiple fronts. A planned "Rate Payer Protection Pledge" would make companies responsible for supplying power to new AI facilities, and several firms — including Microsoft, Google, and Meta — have committed to paying for additions to local electrical grids. Some are going further, constructing private power sources in what has been described as a "shadow grid" strategy. But that approach has already generated controversy: Elon Musk's xAI built methane gas turbines at its Memphis data center that the Environmental Protection Agency ruled were operating illegally without air quality permits, prompting environmental groups to announce plans to sue. The industry is also mobilizing a lobbying campaign, with major operators reportedly planning a "blitz" of targeted advertising and community engagement in regions where they build.

Dan Diorio of the Data Center Coalition argued that the facilities can be a boon to smaller communities, providing tax revenue without straining limited resources. But with more than 40 states offering some form of tax break for data centers, critics increasingly question whether the public is getting a fair deal — setting the stage for a prolonged fight over how and where the infrastructure powering AI's future will be built.

articles.headline: Digital Twin and Bayesian Optimization Slash Particle Accelerator Tuning Time
articles.subheadline: Researchers demonstrate a hybrid approach that cuts tuning iterations by up to sixfold while achieving near-perfect beam transmission
articles.body:
A team of physicists has developed a two-stage tuning strategy for particle accelerators that pairs a computational digital twin with Bayesian optimization, dramatically reducing the time needed to achieve high-quality beam transmission. The work, carried out at the ISAC post-accelerator facility, was detailed in a paper published on the arXiv preprint server on February 23.

The approach, developed by O. Hassan, O. Shelbaya, P. M. Jung, O. Kester, T. Planche, and W. Fedorko, tackles a persistent operational challenge in accelerator physics: the complex, high-dimensional problem of simultaneously tuning beam optics and steering parameters. Rather than treating both tasks as a single optimization problem, the researchers split them apart. A digital twin model first pre-sets the machine's optical configuration, computing end-to-end tunes in a matter of seconds. Bayesian optimization then handles the remaining steering adjustments during live beam operation, all while respecting device bounds, slew-rate limits, and loss interlocks designed to protect the machine.

The results across three experimental case studies were striking. By decoupling optics from steering, the team reduced the number of iterations required to reach high-transmission tunes by a factor of four to six compared to a fully Bayesian baseline that attempted to optimize both optics and steering simultaneously. Final beam transmissions consistently landed in the mid- to high-90 percent range — a benchmark that represents near-optimal performance for such facilities.

Beyond raw speed, the factorized approach offers several practical advantages for day-to-day accelerator operations. Reducing the dimensionality of the parameter space makes convergence more predictable, a crucial property when expensive beam time is at stake. It also simplifies the enforcement of operational safeguards, since the digital twin can be constrained independently of the online optimization loop. The model's direct interface with the facility's control system means the strategy is not merely a theoretical exercise but a deployable operational tool.

The work reflects a broader trend in accelerator physics toward integrating machine learning and model-based techniques into real-time facility operations. As accelerator facilities worldwide push for higher intensities, tighter tolerances, and more efficient use of beam time, hybrid strategies that leverage both physics-based models and data-driven optimization are likely to become standard practice. The ISAC results suggest that the most effective path forward may not be fully autonomous AI-driven tuning, but rather a carefully structured collaboration between computational models and intelligent search algorithms.

articles.headline: Wall Street Rallies Ahead of Nvidia Earnings as AI Optimism Holds
articles.subheadline: Traders bid up equities in anticipation of the chipmaker's results, while Bitcoin climbs past $69,000
articles.body:
U.S. stocks advanced on Monday as investors positioned ahead of Nvidia Corp.'s upcoming earnings report, widely seen as a bellwether for the durability of the artificial intelligence spending boom that has powered markets for the past two years.

The broad rally reflected persistent confidence that corporate investment in AI infrastructure remains robust, despite periodic concerns about stretched valuations across the technology sector. Nvidia, whose graphics processors have become the backbone of AI training and inference workloads, is expected to deliver results that could either validate the current market trajectory or prompt a reassessment of the AI trade that has defined recent market cycles.

Beyond equities, Bitcoin pushed above $69,000, continuing a stretch of strength for the largest cryptocurrency. Meanwhile, a recent rally in U.S. Treasuries lost momentum, with bond prices stalling as traders recalibrated expectations around Federal Reserve policy and the broader macroeconomic outlook.

The pre-earnings positioning underscores just how central Nvidia has become to market sentiment. The chipmaker's quarterly reports have evolved into de facto referendums on the AI industry's trajectory, with implications that ripple well beyond the semiconductor sector into cloud computing, data center infrastructure, and enterprise software. Any signal from management about demand trends, supply constraints, or forward guidance is likely to set the tone for technology stocks in the weeks ahead.

With markets already pricing in continued strength in AI-related spending, the stakes for Nvidia's report are elevated. A beat could reinforce the bullish narrative and push indices toward new highs, while any sign of softening demand or margin pressure risks triggering a broader pullback in a market that has leaned heavily on a handful of mega-cap technology names for its gains.

articles.headline: Google Brings AI-Powered Task Automation to Android via Gemini
articles.subheadline: New beta feature lets Gemini order rides, food, and groceries autonomously — but only on select devices and in limited markets
articles.body:
Google announced on Wednesday a suite of updates to its Gemini AI assistant on Android, headlined by a new capability that allows the AI to execute multi-step tasks such as ordering an Uber or placing a food delivery — effectively turning the assistant into an autonomous agent that can navigate apps on a user's behalf.

The automation feature, launching in beta, is initially restricted to apps in the food, grocery, and rideshare categories, and will only be available on the Pixel 10, Pixel 10 Pro, and Samsung Galaxy S26 series devices. Geographic availability is limited to the United States and South Korea at launch. Google has built in several safeguards: automations require an explicit voice or text command to initiate, users can monitor progress in real time and halt any task mid-execution, and the operations run inside a secure virtual window that is sandboxed from the rest of the device's data.

The release positions Google squarely in an intensifying race among major AI companies to build consumer-facing agents that go beyond answering questions to actually completing tasks. OpenAI's ChatGPT already offers scheduled tasks and a computer-use agent capable of navigating calendars and generating presentations. Anthropic's Cowork feature extends its Claude model to non-coding file and task management. A viral newcomer called OpenClaw has also gained traction for handling everyday chores like email, calendar management, and flight check-ins. Google's approach differs by embedding automation directly into the mobile operating system layer, potentially giving it deeper integration with the Android app ecosystem.

Alongside the automation rollout, Google expanded its Gemini-powered scam detection for phone calls to the Samsung Galaxy S26 series in the U.S., complementing existing availability on Pixel devices across six countries. The company is also deploying on-device scam text detection on Pixel 10 devices in the U.S., Canada, and the U.K., with Galaxy S26 support coming soon. Additionally, Google's Circle to Search feature — which lets users draw gestures on their screen to initiate visual searches — now supports identifying all items visible on the display simultaneously, rather than requiring users to select objects one at a time.

The timing of Google's announcement underscores a widening gap between Android and iOS in the AI assistant race. Apple has repeatedly delayed the launch of its overhauled, AI-powered Siri, most recently pushing the release further into 2026. Google, by contrast, has maintained a steady cadence of Gemini enhancements delivered through operating system updates and its periodic Pixel Drop releases, steadily expanding the assistant's capabilities from conversational tool to autonomous agent.

articles.headline: Tikehau Capital Co-Founder Addresses Rising Private Credit Fears
articles.subheadline: Mounting default warnings and AI-driven disruption concerns rattle investors in the $1.7 trillion private credit market
articles.body:
Tikehau Capital co-founder Mathieu Chabran has waded into the growing debate over risks building in the private credit market, as investors grapple with warnings about rising defaults and the potential for artificial intelligence to reshape the lending landscape.

Speaking on Bloomberg's Open Interest program on February 25, Chabran addressed mounting anxiety among market participants who have watched private credit swell into one of the most prominent corners of alternative finance. The sector, which has ballooned past $1.7 trillion in assets globally, has drawn increasing scrutiny from regulators, ratings agencies, and institutional investors concerned that years of rapid growth may have sown the seeds of future credit losses. With interest rates remaining elevated and economic uncertainty persisting in key markets, the specter of rising defaults has moved from a theoretical worry to a more pressing concern for allocators with heavy exposure to the asset class.

The Paris-based Tikehau Capital, which manages approximately €47 billion in assets across private credit, private equity, real assets, and capital markets strategies, occupies a significant position in European alternative investment management. Chabran's comments come at a moment when the private credit industry faces a confluence of pressures: borrowers who locked in floating-rate loans during the low-rate era are now shouldering substantially higher debt-servicing costs, while the rapid advancement of AI technologies threatens to disrupt business models across the portfolios of direct lenders.

The concern over AI-driven disruption adds a newer dimension to the private credit risk conversation. As artificial intelligence tools accelerate automation and reshape competitive dynamics across industries, lenders face the challenge of underwriting credits in an environment where the long-term viability of borrowers may be harder to assess. Companies that once appeared to occupy defensible market positions could find their revenue streams eroded faster than traditional credit analysis would anticipate.

The broader private credit industry has faced a drumbeat of cautionary voices in recent months, with several prominent asset managers and central bank officials flagging the opacity of the market, the concentration of risk among non-bank lenders, and the potential for valuation marks to lag underlying deterioration in credit quality. For firms like Tikehau, which have built their franchises on the promise of superior risk-adjusted returns through private lending, navigating this environment will require demonstrating that disciplined underwriting can withstand the stresses that critics have long warned about.

articles.headline: CME Group Halts Metals and Natural Gas Trading Over Technical Issues
articles.subheadline: Globex platform outage disrupts futures and options markets as exchange works to resolve problems
articles.body:
CME Group, the world's largest derivatives exchange, suspended trading in metals and natural gas futures and options on its Globex electronic platform on Tuesday due to technical difficulties, the company disclosed in an alert posted to its website.

The halt affected two of the exchange's most actively traded commodity segments, potentially disrupting price discovery and hedging activity for a wide range of market participants — from energy producers and mining companies to institutional traders and speculators. Globex serves as the backbone of CME's nearly around-the-clock electronic trading operations, handling millions of contracts daily across asset classes including equities, interest rates, foreign exchange, and commodities.

The exchange did not immediately specify the nature of the technical problems or provide a timeline for restoring normal operations. CME's metals complex includes benchmark gold, silver, copper, and platinum futures, while its natural gas franchise encompasses Henry Hub futures — the primary pricing reference for U.S. natural gas markets. Any extended disruption to these contracts could ripple across global commodity markets, particularly given the role CME benchmarks play in physical commodity pricing.

Technical outages at major exchanges, while relatively infrequent, can carry significant consequences for traders unable to execute orders or manage risk positions during periods of market volatility. CME Group, which also operates the Chicago Board of Trade and the New York Mercantile Exchange, has invested heavily in technology infrastructure in recent years to minimize such disruptions. The company reported average daily volume of more than 26 million contracts across its platforms in 2025.

Market participants will be closely watching for updates from CME on the cause of the interruption and the timeline for resuming trading. Extended outages have historically drawn scrutiny from regulators, including the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, which oversees derivatives markets in the United States.

articles.headline: Trump Administration Directs Diplomats to Fight Foreign Data Laws
articles.subheadline: A State Department cable signed by Rubio frames data sovereignty rules as obstacles to U.S. AI dominance
articles.body:
The Trump administration has instructed American diplomats worldwide to actively oppose foreign governments' efforts to regulate how U.S. technology companies store and process their citizens' data, according to an internal State Department cable reported by Reuters on February 25.

The directive, signed by Secretary of State Marco Rubio, characterizes data sovereignty laws as threats to artificial intelligence development and global commerce. The cable warns that such regulations would "disrupt global data flows, increase costs and cybersecurity risks, limit AI and cloud services, and expand government control in ways that can undermine civil liberties and enable censorship." Diplomats have been specifically ordered to "counter unnecessarily burdensome regulations, such as data localization mandates" — requirements that force companies to store user data within the borders of the country where it was collected.

Beyond lobbying against proposed rules, the cable tasks diplomats with monitoring and tracking legislative efforts worldwide that would advance data sovereignty frameworks. It also directs them to promote the Global Cross-Border Privacy Rules Forum, an international body that facilitates data transfers through voluntary privacy certifications rather than mandatory localization requirements. The U.S. State Department did not respond to requests for comment.

The directive lands amid a global wave of data regulation. The European Union has established itself as the most aggressive regulator through the General Data Protection Regulation, the Digital Services Act, and the AI Act — a suite of laws designed to limit how technology companies collect, exploit, and profit from user data. Other nations across Asia, Africa, and Latin America have increasingly pursued similar frameworks, motivated by concerns over digital sovereignty and the concentration of data in American corporate infrastructure.

The cable represents the clearest articulation yet of how the administration plans to leverage diplomatic channels to protect the competitive position of U.S. AI and cloud computing firms. Companies like Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and OpenAI depend on the free flow of international data to train AI models and deliver cloud services at scale. Data localization mandates can impose significant infrastructure costs and limit the datasets available for AI training. Critics, however, argue that the approach effectively asks foreign governments to subordinate their citizens' privacy interests to American commercial objectives — a framing that could complicate diplomatic relationships, particularly with allies in Europe who view robust data protection as a fundamental right.

articles.headline: Allianz Announces Up to €2.5 Billion Share Buyback Program
articles.subheadline: German insurance giant returns excess capital to shareholders as CEO Baete continues investor-friendly strategy
articles.body:
Allianz SE, Europe's largest insurer and parent company of bond management powerhouse Pacific Investment Management Co. (PIMCO), has unveiled plans to repurchase up to €2.5 billion of its own shares, signaling continued confidence in its financial position and a commitment to returning surplus capital to investors.

The buyback announcement, disclosed on Tuesday, represents the latest move by Chief Executive Officer Oliver Baete to channel excess cash back to shareholders. Allianz has pursued a consistent capital return strategy in recent years, using a combination of dividends and share repurchases to reward investors as the Munich-based company generates robust cash flows from its insurance and asset management operations.

The program underscores the strong capital position that major European insurers have maintained, even amid a shifting macroeconomic landscape. Allianz, which manages assets across its insurance divisions and through PIMCO — one of the world's largest fixed-income investors — has benefited from higher interest rates that have bolstered investment returns on its vast portfolio. The company's Solvency II ratio, a key measure of financial resilience for European insurers, has remained comfortably above regulatory requirements, giving management room to deploy capital toward shareholder returns.

Share buybacks have become an increasingly popular tool among Europe's blue-chip financial firms, with insurers and banks alike using repurchases to boost earnings per share and signal management's belief that their stock remains attractively valued. For Allianz, the €2.5 billion ceiling on the latest program represents a substantial commitment, reinforcing its position among the continent's most shareholder-friendly companies.

The announcement comes as European insurers broadly navigate a period of solid profitability, supported by disciplined underwriting and favorable investment conditions. Investors will be watching for further details on the timing and execution of the buyback, as well as any signals from Baete's leadership team about the company's broader capital allocation priorities heading deeper into 2026.

articles.headline: US Lends $26.5 Billion to Southern Co. for Power Projects
articles.subheadline: Trump administration closes massive financing package as data center boom strains grid capacity and affordability
articles.body:
The Trump administration finalized a $26.5 billion loan package for Southern Company power projects in Georgia and Alabama on Wednesday, marking one of the largest federal energy financing deals in recent memory as the nation grapples with surging electricity demand and growing concerns over affordability.

The loan, closed by the U.S. Department of Energy, is directed at bolstering power generation infrastructure operated by subsidiaries of Southern Company, one of the largest electric utilities in the United States. The deal comes at a moment when the American power grid faces unprecedented strain from the rapid proliferation of electricity-hungry data centers, driven largely by the artificial intelligence boom, as well as broader electrification trends across the economy.

Rising electricity costs have become a political flashpoint, with consumers and regulators alike raising alarms about the affordability of keeping the lights on. Data centers alone are projected to consume a rapidly growing share of U.S. electricity output in the coming years, and utilities across the Southeast — a region that has attracted significant investment from hyperscale cloud and AI operators — are scrambling to build out generation capacity to meet the demand. Southern Company, headquartered in Atlanta, serves approximately nine million customers across several southeastern states and has been at the center of these expansion efforts.

The scale of the federal financing underscores the administration's willingness to deploy substantial government capital to accelerate energy infrastructure buildout, even as debates continue over the appropriate mix of generation sources. Southern Company's portfolio includes nuclear, natural gas, and renewable assets, and the company has been a key player in the development of the Vogtle nuclear expansion in Georgia — a project that experienced significant cost overruns and delays before its final units came online.

The loan package is likely to face scrutiny from fiscal hawks and energy policy analysts who will examine whether the terms adequately protect taxpayers. But proponents argue that federal financing is essential to keeping pace with electricity demand that threatens to outstrip supply, potentially leading to reliability crises and further price increases for households and businesses across the region.

articles.headline: Blockfills CEO Steps Down as Crypto Lender Reels From $75M Loss
articles.subheadline: Chicago-based firm froze client withdrawals earlier this month and is now seeking a buyer amid broader market weakness
articles.body:
Nicholas Hammer, co-founder of institutional crypto lender Blockfills, has exited the chief executive role as the firm grapples with approximately $75 million in lending losses and a freeze on customer deposits and withdrawals that has persisted since February 11. Joseph Perry has been named interim CEO, according to the company's website.

The leadership shakeup at the Chicago-based firm, which processed more than $60 billion in trading volume in 2025 — a 28 percent increase year over year — underscores the fragility that still haunts crypto lending even as the industry matures. A person with direct knowledge of the situation told CoinDesk that some clients were encouraged to pull their assets from the platform before the withdrawal halt took effect, raising questions about whether all customers received equal treatment. Blockfills is now actively seeking a buyer, according to earlier reporting.

The episode carries uncomfortable echoes of the 2022 crypto winter, when Celsius, BlockFi, and Genesis each froze customer accounts as cascading losses tore through the sector. Blockfills serves roughly 2,000 institutional clients — hedge funds, asset managers, and mining operations among them — and counts Susquehanna Private Equity Investments, CME Ventures, and Nexo among its backers. The firm raised $37 million in a Series A round in January 2022.

Blockfills said in a February 11 statement that it was working with investors and clients to restore liquidity and that customers could still open and close positions in spot and derivatives markets. But as of publication, deposit and withdrawal capabilities remain suspended, leaving client funds in limbo. Hammer did not respond to requests for comment, and his LinkedIn profile still listed him as CEO as of February 25.

The turmoil arrives during a difficult stretch for digital assets more broadly. Bitcoin has remained below $70,000 following a sharp retreat from its late-2025 record high, while ether trades near $2,000, reflecting cautious sentiment across the market. The Blockfills situation will likely intensify calls for stronger risk management standards and regulatory oversight in institutional crypto lending — a segment that has repeatedly proved vulnerable to sudden market dislocations despite its growing scale.

articles.headline: Jamie Dimon Warns Rivals Are Repeating Pre-2008 'Dumb Things' in Credit
articles.subheadline: JPMorgan CEO's concerns about reckless lending echo financial crisis parallels as macro analysts back his assessment
articles.body:
JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon has issued a pointed warning that competitors are engaging in reckless behavior in the credit markets, drawing explicit parallels to the kind of risk-taking that precipitated the 2008 global financial crisis.

Dimon, who has led the largest U.S. bank since 2005 and navigated it through the last major financial meltdown, said rivals are doing "dumb things" in credit — a characterization that Bloomberg macro strategist Simon White described as well founded. The comments come at a time when credit spreads remain historically tight and leveraged lending has surged, conditions that some analysts view as signs of complacency among market participants chasing yield in an increasingly competitive landscape.

The JPMorgan chief's warnings carry particular weight given his track record. During the lead-up to the 2008 crisis, Dimon was among the few major bank CEOs who recognized early signs of excess in mortgage-related credit products. His bank emerged from that episode in a stronger competitive position than most peers, in part because of a more disciplined approach to risk. Now, with private credit markets having ballooned past $1.7 trillion and traditional banks competing aggressively for lending business, Dimon appears to see a familiar pattern forming — one where the pressure to maintain market share leads institutions to underwrite deals with deteriorating terms and insufficient safeguards.

White's analysis supports the thesis that current credit market dynamics warrant concern. When spreads compress to levels that no longer adequately compensate for default risk, and when covenant protections erode in loan agreements, the system becomes more fragile and vulnerable to sudden repricing. The danger is not necessarily an imminent repeat of 2008, but rather that accumulated risk-taking could amplify the next economic downturn whenever it arrives.

For investors and regulators alike, Dimon's remarks serve as a reminder that periods of apparent market calm often mask the buildup of systemic vulnerabilities. Whether his peers heed the warning — or dismiss it as competitive posturing — may not be clear until the next credit cycle turns.

articles.headline: UBS Wealth CIO: Investors Should Pivot From Coders to Builders
articles.subheadline: AI disruption threatens software stocks; miners, power producers, and industrials stand to benefit, top strategist says
articles.body:
Investors should rotate out of software-heavy technology stocks and into companies that operate in the physical world — miners, power producers, and industrial firms — as artificial intelligence increasingly threatens to disrupt digital business models, according to a top UBS Wealth Management strategist.

Ulrike Hoffmann-Burchardi, global head of equities and chief investment officer for the Americas at UBS Wealth Management, argued that AI's rapid advance poses a material risk to software-backed businesses, many of which have commanded premium valuations for years on the assumption of durable competitive moats. As AI tools grow more capable of writing code, automating workflows, and replicating software functionality, those moats may prove far shallower than investors have priced in.

The recommendation marks a notable shift in tone from one of the world's largest wealth managers. For much of the past decade, the prevailing institutional view favored asset-light technology companies over capital-intensive industries. Hoffmann-Burchardi's thesis flips that calculus: the very physicality of mining, energy generation, and industrial manufacturing — the difficulty of replicating a copper mine or a power plant with a large language model — becomes a source of durable value rather than a drag on returns.

The argument also dovetails with broader market trends. Surging demand for data centers and AI infrastructure has already driven a re-rating of power utilities and energy companies, while industrial firms supplying the physical backbone of the AI buildout have seen renewed investor interest. At the same time, several high-flying software names have faced valuation compression as the market reassesses which businesses AI will augment and which it will commoditize.

For allocators, the message from UBS is straightforward: in an era where code can be generated at near-zero marginal cost, scarcity resides in atoms, not bits. Whether the market fully embraces that rotation remains to be seen, but the strategic case for real-world builders over digital incumbents is gaining influential advocates.

articles.headline: Crypto Super PAC That Pledged $100M for 2026 Midterms Has Zero Funds
articles.subheadline: Fellowship PAC announced with fanfare last September, but FEC filings show no money raised as primaries approach
articles.body:
A crypto-focused political action committee that drew widespread attention last September with a splashy promise of $100 million in campaign spending for the 2026 U.S. midterm elections has yet to raise a single dollar, according to Federal Election Commission filings.

The Fellowship PAC launched with a press release praising President Donald Trump's regulatory approach to digital assets and pledging to back "pro-innovation, pro-crypto candidates." Early reports linked stablecoin giant Tether as a potential backer, but the company this week distanced itself from the effort. "Tether International has no affiliation or oversight of Fellowship," spokesperson Alex Welch told CoinDesk, though she did not address whether Tether's newer U.S. operations had any connection. The PAC's original announcement identified no officers, donors, or key employees, and repeated attempts to contact the organization have gone unanswered.

The only visible thread connecting Fellowship to the broader crypto ecosystem is its FEC registration, which lists Mitchell Nobel as treasurer. Nobel serves as director of digital-assets strategy at Cantor Fitzgerald, the financial firm formerly led by Howard Lutnick — now Trump's Secretary of Commerce — and which has managed Tether's assets in recent years. Fellowship maintains a website and an account on X, where its most recent activity was reposting a comment from Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino earlier this month.

The PAC's dormancy raises questions about whether foreign-money restrictions may have complicated its plans. Under U.S. election law, political action committees cannot accept funding from non-U.S. entities. Any involvement from Tether — headquartered outside the United States — would have required that funds originate domestically and that political decisions not be guided by foreign nationals, a standard that has drawn heightened scrutiny during the Trump administration.

Meanwhile, the crypto industry's dominant political player, Fairshake, is already deploying resources. The super PAC — primarily funded by Coinbase, Andreessen Horowitz, and Ripple — has reported $193 million on hand and has begun targeting early 2026 races. In the 2024 election cycle, Fairshake and its affiliates backed more than 50 successful congressional candidates from both parties, demonstrating that nine-figure political spending can meaningfully shape crypto policy outcomes. With some of the earliest 2026 primaries fast approaching, any new entrant to the political spending arena risks arriving too late to matter.

articles.headline: Trump's Record-Length State of the Union Draws Sweeping Fact-Check Challenges
articles.subheadline: BBC Verify analysis finds multiple misleading or unsupported claims across inflation, gas prices, investment, and foreign policy
articles.body:
President Donald Trump's State of the Union address — at one hour and 47 minutes, the longest in decades — painted a triumphant picture of his administration's first year back in power, but independent fact-checkers found several of his marquee claims to be misleading, exaggerated, or lacking evidence.

On the economy, Trump told Congress that his policies are "rapidly ending" high prices created under his predecessor. While it is true that the annual inflation rate has eased to 2.4% as of January 2026, down from 3% during President Biden's final year, the picture is more complicated than Trump suggested. Beef prices, which he singled out as falling "significantly," actually rose 15% over the prior 12 months, with only a slight dip in the most recent month. Economists at Harvard University have estimated that Trump's own tariff regime added roughly 0.92 percentage points to consumer price inflation in January 2026. His claim that gasoline is "below $2.30 a gallon in most states" was particularly difficult to square with reality: the national average stood at $2.95 per gallon according to the AAA, with only Oklahoma averaging near $2.37 — the lowest in the country.

Trump's assertion that he had secured "more than $18 trillion" in investment commitments found no public corroboration. The White House's own investment tracker lists $9.7 trillion in pledges — a substantial figure, but barely half the president's claim. Greg Auclair of the Peterson Institute for International Economics cautioned that even tracked commitments "include pledges that may not materialise," pointing to a stalled EU trade deal as one example. Trump's statement that "more Americans are working today than at any time in history" is technically accurate — employment topped 158 million in January — but the employment-to-population ratio actually slipped from 60.1% to 59.8% since Biden left office, and unemployment edged up from 4.1% to 4.3%.

Foreign policy claims also drew scrutiny. Trump declared he had "ended eight wars" in his first ten months, but analysis showed that several of the conflicts listed by the White House lasted only days and one — a dispute between Egypt and Ethiopia over dam construction — involved no military combat at all. Fighting has resumed between Rwanda and the Democratic Republic of Congo despite a Washington-brokered agreement, and India publicly downplayed the U.S. role in resolving its brief clash with Pakistan. Trump's involvement in the Israel-Hamas ceasefire is his strongest claim to peacemaking credit.

On immigration, Trump's statement that "zero illegal aliens have been admitted" in nine months is accurate in the narrow sense that Border Patrol has not released any migrants from custody, according to the Department of Homeland Security. However, authorities still recorded 6,070 apprehensions at the southwest border in January 2026. His renewed assertion that electoral cheating is "rampant" remains unsupported: the Heritage Foundation's database documents just 1,620 proven fraud cases nationwide since 1982, and a Brennan Center study of 23.5 million votes in 2016 found only 30 suspected instances of non-citizen voting referred for investigation.

articles.headline: Diageo Shares Suffer Record Plunge as New CEO Signals Deep Turnaround
articles.subheadline: Incoming chief Dave Lewis cuts outlook, warning investors there is no quick fix for the British spirits giant
articles.body:
Diageo Plc suffered its largest single-day share decline on record on Tuesday after new Chief Executive Officer Dave Lewis delivered a sobering assessment of the challenges facing the world's biggest spirits maker, cutting the company's outlook and signaling that a meaningful recovery will take time.

The London-listed drinks giant, whose portfolio spans Johnnie Walker scotch, Guinness stout, and Tanqueray gin, saw its stock tumble as Lewis laid bare the depth of restructuring required. The sharp sell-off reflected investor dismay at the scale of problems accumulated at a company long considered one of Europe's most reliable consumer goods performers. Lewis, who built his reputation as a turnaround specialist during his tenure leading Tesco through its own crisis years ago, made clear that the path back to consistent growth would demand patience.

Diageo has been grappling with a confluence of headwinds in recent quarters, including softening demand in key markets, destocking by distributors, and shifting consumer preferences that have pressured volumes across several of its core spirits categories. The outlook cut suggests these pressures are proving more persistent than previously acknowledged by the company's leadership. Lewis's willingness to reset expectations early in his tenure follows a well-worn playbook for incoming CEOs — taking the pain upfront to establish a lower baseline from which future performance can be measured.

The record decline underscores just how far Diageo's standing has fallen among investors who once prized the stock for its defensive qualities and dependable dividend growth. The company's market capitalization has eroded significantly from its pandemic-era highs, when a boom in at-home drinking and premiumization trends propelled spirits sales to record levels. The subsequent normalization has been more painful than many analysts anticipated.

For Lewis, the immediate task will be convincing shareholders that Diageo's brand portfolio and global distribution network remain fundamentally strong assets, even as near-term financial performance disappoints. The market's reaction suggests that confidence is in short supply, and the new CEO faces a long road to rebuild it.

articles.headline: UK Commons Speaker Gave Police Flight Risk Tip Before Mandelson Arrest
articles.subheadline: Sir Lindsay Hoyle says he acted 'in good faith' passing information about the former ambassador's travel plans
articles.body:
Sir Lindsay Hoyle, the Speaker of the House of Commons, confirmed on Tuesday that he provided the Metropolitan Police with information suggesting Lord Peter Mandelson could be a flight risk — a tip that preceded the former Labour minister's arrest on Monday as part of a misconduct in public office investigation linked to Jeffrey Epstein.

Speaking before MPs after the Times first reported his involvement, Sir Lindsay said he passed on "relevant" information "in good faith" and considered it his "duty and responsibility" to do so. The BBC reported that Hoyle received intelligence during a visit to the British Virgin Islands last week indicating Mandelson was planning to travel there. Sir Lindsay expressed regret that the matter had quickly leaked to the media but offered no further details about the nature or source of his information.

The disclosure has injected a new layer of political intrigue into an already extraordinary case. Mandelson's legal team at Mishcon de Reya said their client had already agreed to a voluntary police interview next month, making the arrest unnecessary. They characterized the flight risk claim as "baseless" and have written to the Met demanding to know what evidence underpinned the decision to take him into custody. Adding to the confusion, Mandelson's lawyers said they were told twice by police that the referral came from Lord Forsyth, the Speaker of the House of Lords — a claim Forsyth has categorically denied as "entirely false and without foundation." The Met has declined to comment on the matter.

Mandelson was taken to Wandsworth police station in London on Monday afternoon and later released on bail, surrendering his passport as a condition. The investigation centers on allegations that, while serving as a government minister, he passed market-sensitive information to Epstein, the late convicted sex offender and financier. The allegations surfaced after the U.S. Department of Justice released a cache of documents last month, including emails between the two men. Mandelson, who was appointed British ambassador to the United States in February 2025, was dismissed from the post in September after Downing Street said new information had emerged about the depth of his relationship with Epstein.

Mandelson's legal team has maintained that he committed no criminal acts, was not motivated by financial gain, and intends to cooperate fully with investigators to "clear his name." The Metropolitan Police is understood to have conducted its own assessment of the credibility of the information Hoyle provided before proceeding with the arrest, though the force has offered no public explanation for its decision-making.

articles.headline: Physicists Uncover Fundamental Flaw in Wave Superposition for Layered Media
articles.subheadline: New research shows the superposition principle can fail for evanescent waves, then offers a fix that restores convergence
articles.body:
A pair of physicists has demonstrated that the superposition principle — one of the bedrock assumptions of linear wave physics — can break down when applied to multilayered media containing evanescent and inhomogeneous waves. The finding, published as a preprint on arXiv on February 17, challenges a foundational tool used across optics, acoustics, and electromagnetic theory.

Michael Mazilu and Andriejus Demčenko showed that when fields in layered systems are expanded as infinite series of evanescent wave components, the resulting superposition can diverge rather than converge to a physical solution. The problem emerges specifically in systems with three or more interfaces and arises because evanescent waves — fields that decay exponentially rather than propagate — cannot be properly normalized within the conventional mathematical basis. Crucially, the authors stress this is not a numerical artifact or computational error but a genuine mathematical failure of the standard representation.

The breakdown was identified using two well-established formalisms: the Airy formula, which describes multiple reflections within layered structures, and the scattering-matrix (S-matrix) approach widely used in photonics and condensed matter physics. In both frameworks, the researchers pinpointed the conditions under which partial-wave expansions involving evanescent modes produce divergent series, rendering standard analytical techniques unreliable.

To remedy the problem, Mazilu and Demčenko introduce what they call "power flux modes" — an orthonormal basis of wave solutions constructed to preserve energy conservation during scattering events. They prove that in this flux-orthonormal basis, scattering at each interface becomes unitary and propagation eigenvalues remain bounded, which together guarantee that series expansions converge. The approach eliminates the need for ad hoc regularization or renormalization procedures that physicists and engineers have sometimes employed when encountering divergent evanescent-mode sums.

The framework applies broadly across scalar, electromagnetic, and elastic wave systems, suggesting potential implications for fields ranging from thin-film optics and semiconductor device modeling to seismology and ultrasonic nondestructive testing. Multilayered structures are ubiquitous in modern technology — from anti-reflection coatings and photonic crystals to acoustic metamaterials — making a rigorous treatment of evanescent mode convergence practically significant.

While the paper is currently posted in the General Physics section of arXiv and has not yet undergone formal peer review, it addresses a subtle but important gap in the mathematical foundations of wave physics that has persisted largely unexamined. If validated, the power flux mode framework could become a standard tool for any analysis involving evanescent fields in stratified systems.

articles.headline: Bitcoin Surges Past $67,500 as Massive Short Squeeze Unwinds
articles.subheadline: Over $307 million in bearish bets liquidated in 24 hours as crypto market snaps weeks of extreme fear
articles.body:
Bitcoin rallied more than 5% to approximately $67,500 on Wednesday, catalyzing a broad relief rally across digital assets after weeks of deeply negative sentiment and crowded short positions that left the market primed for a sharp reversal.

The move liquidated over $307 million in leveraged bearish bets across crypto derivatives in just 24 hours, according to CoinGlass data. Major altcoins outpaced bitcoin's gains, with ether climbing roughly 10% to reclaim the $2,000 level for the first time in a week, while solana, dogecoin, cardano, and chainlink each posted advances exceeding 10%. The rally came after the widely watched Crypto Fear & Greed Index spent most of February mired in "Extreme Fear" territory, and perpetual futures funding rates had repeatedly turned negative — a telltale sign that bearish positioning had become overcrowded.

Crypto-related equities followed suit. Stablecoin issuer Circle surged 20% after beating earnings estimates, while Coinbase, Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy), and Galaxy Digital each gained between 5% and 6%. Bitcoin mining stocks including Bitfarms, Bitdeer, and MARA Holdings extended their own rebounds. Markus Thielen of 10x Research noted that many crypto-linked stocks had accumulated sizable short interest from hedge funds, setting the stage for the sharp upside reversal.

Perhaps more significant than the price action itself were signs that U.S. institutional buyers may be re-entering the market. The Coinbase Premium Index — which tracks the price gap between bitcoin on Coinbase and the global average and serves as a proxy for American capital flows — turned positive for the first time in over 40 days. Meanwhile, U.S. spot bitcoin ETFs recorded $257.7 million in inflows on Tuesday, the largest single-day total since early February. Notably, bitcoin perpetual funding rates remained below neutral even during the rally, suggesting the bounce was not driven by aggressive new leveraged speculation.

The crypto rebound unfolded against a supportive backdrop in traditional markets, with the S&P 500 up 0.6% and the Nasdaq 100 gaining 1.1% in early trading. Still, bitcoin remains down roughly 25% on the year, and whether the current move represents a durable trend change or merely a mechanical unwinding of extreme bearish positioning remains an open question. The speed and breadth of the rally, however, suggest that the pain trade has decisively shifted against the shorts — at least for now.

articles.headline: US to Raise Tariffs to 15% 'Where Appropriate,' Trade Rep Greer Says
articles.subheadline: Trump set to sign supplemental proclamation as administration signals continuity in trade policy
articles.body:
U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer announced on Tuesday that President Donald Trump intends to sign a supplemental proclamation raising tariff rates to 15 percent in cases deemed appropriate, signaling an escalation in the administration's trade agenda.

Greer framed the move as part of the administration's broader push for "continuity" in its tariff strategy, suggesting the increase is designed to maintain pressure on trading partners and reinforce existing trade measures rather than mark a dramatic shift in policy direction. The supplemental proclamation would give the president authority to selectively raise duties on targeted imports, though Greer did not specify which goods or countries would face the higher rate.

The announcement comes as the Trump administration continues to wield tariffs as a central instrument of economic and foreign policy. Since returning to office, Trump has pursued an aggressive trade posture, imposing and threatening levies across a range of sectors and trading partners. A move to 15 percent on select imports would represent a meaningful step up from some existing rates and could affect supply chains, consumer prices, and diplomatic relationships depending on the scope of the proclamation.

Market participants and trade analysts will be closely watching for details on which product categories and countries fall under the "where appropriate" designation. The language suggests the administration is preserving flexibility to apply the higher tariff selectively, potentially as leverage in ongoing or future trade negotiations. Industries reliant on imported materials and components could face increased costs, which historically have been passed along to consumers or absorbed through thinning margins.

The timing and specifics of the supplemental proclamation have not yet been disclosed. Trading partners are likely to respond with their own assessments of the measure, and retaliatory actions remain a possibility depending on the breadth of the tariff increase.

articles.headline: Chemists Synthesize 'Impossible' Silicon Aromatic Molecule After 50 Years
articles.subheadline: Pentasilacyclopentadienide, a five-atom silicon ring with full aromatic stability, could reshape catalysis and materials science
articles.body:
A team at Saarland University in Germany has created a silicon-based aromatic molecule that chemists worldwide spent nearly five decades trying — and failing — to produce. The compound, pentasilacyclopentadienide, replaces all five carbon atoms in a classic aromatic ring with silicon, achieving a level of stability long considered theoretically possible but practically out of reach. The results were published in the journal Science on February 24.

David Scheschkewitz, professor of general and inorganic chemistry at Saarland University, led the effort alongside doctoral student Ankur and crystallographer Bernd Morgenstern. Their synthesis marks only the second known class of silicon-based aromatic compound. The first — a three-membered silicon ring analogous to cyclopropenium — was produced in 1981. Every subsequent attempt to build larger all-silicon aromatic systems had ended in failure, making the five-membered ring a kind of white whale for inorganic chemists.

Aromaticity is one of the most consequential phenomena in chemistry. Aromatic molecules feature electrons shared evenly across a flat ring structure, obeying a quantum mechanical counting rule formulated by physicist Erich Hückel. That delocalization grants the molecules exceptional thermodynamic stability, which is why aromatic carbon compounds already underpin vast swaths of industrial chemistry — from plastics catalysis in polyethylene and polypropylene production to pharmaceutical synthesis. Achieving the same aromatic stability with silicon is significant because the element behaves differently from carbon at a fundamental level: it is more metallic and holds its electrons less tightly, properties that could yield catalysts and materials with entirely novel characteristics.

In a notable coincidence, a group led by Takeaki Iwamoto at Tohoku University in Sendai, Japan, independently synthesized the same compound at almost the same time. The two teams agreed to publish their findings side by side in the same issue of Science, lending additional confidence to the result and underscoring the compound's importance to the field.

The practical implications remain to be explored, but researchers say the breakthrough opens a new frontier. Silicon aromatic compounds could serve as ligands for catalysts with tunable electronic properties, or as building blocks for advanced materials that have no carbon-based analogue. After decades in which the molecule existed only as a theoretical curiosity, its physical realization gives chemists a concrete platform from which to investigate an unexplored region of the periodic table's chemistry.

articles.headline: Senate Probe Targets Binance Over Alleged $1.7B in Iran-Linked Transfers
articles.subheadline: Sen. Blumenthal demands records on suspicious flows to sanctioned entities and the firing of internal investigators who flagged them
articles.body:
Senator Richard Blumenthal, the ranking Democrat on the Senate Homeland Security Committee, has launched a formal inquiry into Binance over allegations that $1.7 billion in cryptocurrency was routed through the exchange to organizations linked to Iran, including Yemen's Houthi militants.

In a letter sent Tuesday to Binance co-chief executive Richard Teng, Blumenthal requested records pertaining to the exchange's dealings with two Hong Kong-based entities identified as the origin of the suspect transfers. One of the accounts was reportedly registered to Blessed Trust, a Hong Kong firm that operated as a Binance vendor. The senator also demanded documentation on the suspension and dismissal of compliance staff who initially uncovered the alleged violations — a detail that has drawn scrutiny from multiple news outlets including the New York Times, Wall Street Journal, and Fortune. "Binance appears to have ignored warnings and recommendations to prevent Iranian money laundering schemes on its cryptocurrency exchange," Blumenthal wrote.

Binance has firmly denied the allegations, calling prior reporting inaccurate and insisting it maintains strict know-your-customer and compliance protocols. "There are no Iranian users on the platform," a company spokesperson said, adding that claims about retaliatory firings of investigators are false. The exchange said it canceled the flagged accounts and severed ties with Blessed Trust in January. In a blog post over the weekend, Binance characterized its "sanctions-related exposure" as minimal and disclosed that an ongoing internal review would be submitted to the U.S. Justice Department by February 25.

The probe reopens a fraught chapter in Binance's regulatory history. In November 2023, founder and former CEO Changpeng Zhao pleaded guilty to violating anti-money-laundering laws and facilitating transactions with customers in sanctioned nations, including Iran. Binance agreed to pay $4.3 billion in penalties and exit the U.S. market as part of the settlement. Zhao served four months in prison before receiving a pardon from President Donald Trump.

The Senate investigation adds a new layer of political and legal risk for the world's largest crypto exchange at a time when the broader digital asset market remains under pressure. If the allegations are substantiated, Binance could face additional enforcement actions that would test the durability of its 2023 settlement with U.S. authorities and raise fresh questions about the effectiveness of compliance reforms implemented under its current leadership.

articles.headline: Engineered Bacteria Designed to Devour Tumors From the Inside Out
articles.subheadline: University of Waterloo team programs soil microbes with DNA 'circuits' to colonize and consume solid cancers
articles.body:
Researchers at the University of Waterloo have engineered bacteria capable of infiltrating the oxygen-starved cores of solid tumors and consuming them from within, a synthetic biology approach that could open an entirely new front in cancer treatment.

The strategy centers on Clostridium sporogenes, a common soil bacterium that thrives exclusively in oxygen-free environments. Because the interior of many solid tumors consists of dead cells with virtually no oxygen, these microbes find an ideal habitat once introduced as spores. "Bacteria spores enter the tumor, finding an environment where there are lots of nutrients and no oxygen, which this organism prefers, and so it starts eating those nutrients and growing in size," said Marc Aucoin, a chemical engineering professor at Waterloo. The bacterium effectively colonizes the tumor's core and begins dismantling it.

The central engineering challenge has been what happens at the tumor's edges, where traces of oxygen persist. As the bacteria expand outward from the core, they encounter enough oxygen to kill them off before they can eliminate the full mass of cancerous tissue. To overcome this, the Waterloo team inserted a gene from a related, more oxygen-tolerant bacterium — but they needed a safety mechanism to prevent it from activating prematurely. Bacteria circulating in the oxygen-rich bloodstream with that gene switched on could pose serious risks to patients. The solution was quorum sensing, a natural bacterial communication system in which microbes release chemical signals that intensify as their population grows. Only after a critical mass of bacteria has accumulated inside a tumor does the signal reach the threshold needed to activate the oxygen-resistance gene. The result is a tightly controlled biological trigger.

In work published in ACS Synthetic Biology, the team demonstrated this quorum-sensing circuit by programming bacteria to produce a green fluorescent protein at the intended activation point, confirming the system's reliability. "Using synthetic biology, we built something like an electrical circuit, but instead of wires we used pieces of DNA," said Brian Ingalls, a professor of applied mathematics at Waterloo. "Each piece has its job. When assembled correctly, they form a system that works in a predictable way." The next milestone is combining the oxygen-tolerance gene and the quorum-sensing control mechanism into a single organism and testing it against tumors in pre-clinical trials.

The research grew out of doctoral work by Bahram Zargar, now co-founder of Toronto-based CREM Co Labs, and was significantly advanced by former Waterloo PhD student Sara Sadr. The project reflects a broader push in oncology toward programmable biological therapeutics — treatments that can be designed with logic gates and feedback loops, much like software, but built from living components.
